translatedText: Created masterpiece haiku such as 'Inochi futatsu no naka ni ikitaru sakura kana' and 'Kumo oriori hito o yasumuru tsukimi kana'
Created masterpiece haiku 'Furuike ya kawazu tobikomu mizu no oto'
Died (November 28, lunar calendar October 12)
Recognition of Haikai-no-Renga style
His works were recognized in the collaborative Haikai-no-Renga style during his lifetime.
Master of Haiku
He is considered a master of haiku, which was called hokku in his time.
Internationally renowned poet
His poems are internationally renowned, and most of his poems in Japan have been reproduced on monuments and historical sites.
Fame throughout Japan
He began writing poetry at a young age, and by integrating himself into the intellectual landscape of Edo, he became famous throughout Japan.
Inspiration through travel
He wandered throughout Japan, including the north, east, and west, to get inspiration for his works.
Poetry based on natural experiences
His poetry was influenced by direct experience of the world around him, compressing emotions about the landscape into simple elements.
Who is Matsuo Basho?

Matsuo Basho was a poet of the early Edo shogunate who was born in 1644 and died on November 28, 1694 (lunar calendar October 12). His childhood name was Kinsaku, and he was also called Matsuo Chuemon Munefusa.
What are the main achievements of Matsuo Basho?

He was recognized for his works in the collaborative Haikai-no-Renga style during his lifetime, and today he is considered a master of haiku. His poems are internationally renowned and reproduced on monuments and historical sites throughout Japan.
What are the representative works of Matsuo Basho?

His representative works include the 1685 pieces 'Inochi futatsu no naka ni ikitaru sakura kana', 'Kumo oriori hito o yasumuru tsukimi kana', and the 1686 piece 'Furuike ya kawazu tobikomu mizu no oto'.
In what way did Matsuo Basho do his work?

While making a living as a teacher, he escaped from the social and urban life of the literary circles by wandering around Japan for inspiration. His poetry was influenced by direct experiences of the world around him, compressing emotions about the landscape.
Which of his works did Matsuo Basho consider most important?
He believed that his best work lay in leading and participating in renku, and he valued the connection with haikai syntax more than the hokku itself.
François-Dominique Toussaint (Toussaint Louverture) was born
Black slaves who heard the news of the French Revolution demanded the application of the principles of "Liberty, Equality, Fraternity" to Saint-Domingue
August 22, 1791
A slave revolution broke out in the French colony of Saint-Domingue, and Toussaint became one of its leaders
Led the negotiation for France to abolish slavery in the colonies
August 1793
Succeeded in defending against the invasions of British and Spanish forces
February 4, 1794
The National Convention of the French First Republic declared the abolition of black slavery in all colonies
April 1796
Ruled the colony with the consent of the French governor
Promulgated a constitution forever abolishing slavery and declaring all men equal regardless of skin color
Napoleon I sent a massive army to retake control of the colony and re-establish slavery
Late 1802
Toussaint responded to peace talks but was captured
April 7, 1803
Died in prison while incarcerated in France
People who inherited Toussaint's spirit liberated Port-au-Prince and adopted the Declaration of Independence
The Republic of Haiti was declared
Led the Haitian Independence Movement
He ended slavery and led the independence movement of Saint-Domingue (now Haiti) from France.
Served as French Army General and Colonial Ruler
He served as a general in the French army and colonial ruler of Saint-Domingue.
Led the Slave Revolution
He was one of the leaders of the 1791 slave revolution in Saint-Domingue.
Led Negotiations for the Abolition of Slavery
He negotiated with France to abolish slavery in the colonies on the condition of defeating the British and Spanish forces.
Promulgated Constitution and Declared Abolition of Slavery
In 1801, he promulgated a constitution to abolish slavery forever and declare that all men are equal regardless of skin color.
Who is Toussaint Louverture?
A Haitian revolutionary and freedom fighter, he ended slavery and led the independence movement of Saint-Domingue (now Haiti) from France.
What is Toussaint Louverture's real name?
It is François-Dominique Toussaint.
How did Toussaint Louverture become a revolutionary?
He became a revolutionary by teaching himself French and reading books by Enlightenment thinkers like Voltaire.
When did the slave revolution led by Toussaint Louverture take place?
It occurred on August 22, 1791, in the French colony of Saint-Domingue.
How did Toussaint Louverture die?
In late 1802, he was captured after accepting a peace talk proposal from a French commander, imprisoned in France, and died in prison on April 7, 1803.
What happened to Haiti after Toussaint Louverture's death?

Jean-Jacques Dessalines became a revolutionary and carried on Toussaint's spirit. In 1803, he liberated Port-au-Prince, adopted the Declaration of Independence, and declared the Republic of Haiti in 1804.
Went to study in Spain to become a doctor
Finished the final draft of his novel "Noli Me Tángere" in Berlin in February
Formed La Liga Filipina, a leading organization for the Philippine independence movement
Arrested by the Spanish Governor-General during La Liga Filipina activities and exiled to Dapitan, Mindanao
Accused of orchestrating the Philippine Revolution, he was publicly executed by a firing squad of the Spanish colonial army in Bagumbayan, near Manila, on December 30
Authored "Noli Me Tángere"
He wrote the novel "Noli Me Tángere", criticizing the contradictions of colonial rule and depicting the discrimination against Filipinos by Spaniards.
Formed the Philippine National League
He formed the Philippine National League to criticize Spanish colonial rule out of a desire for the liberation of the Philippines.
Formed La Liga Filipina
He directed the independence movement by forming La Liga Filipina, a leading organization for the Philippine independence movement.
Who is José Rizal?
He is a doctor, writer, poet, journalist, educator, and a Philippine independence activist.
Why was José Rizal executed?
In 1896, he was accused of orchestrating the Philippine Revolution and was publicly executed by a firing squad of the Spanish colonial army.
What are the representative works of José Rizal?
He is the author of "Noli Me Tángere" and "El Filibusterismo", and left behind many poems and essays.
How is José Rizal remembered in the Philippines?
He is revered as the father of Philippine independence, and the day of his death, December 30, is commemorated as Rizal Day.
April 27, 1759
Born as the second child to Edward John Wollstonecraft and Elizabeth Dickson in Spitalfields, London
Became independent and worked as a companion to Sarah Dawson in Bath, Somerset
Returned home to attend to her dying mother
Persuaded her sister Eliza, who was suffering from postpartum depression, to leave her household
Left her school and followed her friend Fanny Blood to Lisbon to nurse her
Published "Thoughts on the Education of Daughters"; decided to become a writer
Published her first novel, "Mary: A Fiction"; published her only children's book, "Original Stories from Real Life"
Wrote "A Vindication of the Rights of Men" in response to Edmund Burke's "Reflections on the Revolution in France"
Wrote her most famous and influential work, "A Vindication of the Rights of Woman"; left for Paris in December, arriving a month before the execution of Louis XVI
February 1793
France declared war on Britain
March 1793
The Jacobin-led Committee of Public Safety seized power in France
Fell in love with American adventurer Gilbert Imlay; Imlay falsely registered Wollstonecraft as his wife so she could obtain US citizenship
Gave birth to her first daughter, Fanny Imlay
July 1794
Fall of the Jacobins
December 1794
"An Historical and Moral View of the French Revolution", written in Le Havre, Northern France, was published in London
April 7, 1795
Left France
April 1795
Returned to London looking for Imlay, but he refused to reconcile
May 1795
Attempted suicide with laudanum, but Imlay saved her life
The letters written to Imlay during her travels in Scandinavia were collected and published as "Letters Written During a Short Residence in Sweden, Norway, and Denmark"
March 29, 1797
Moved to Somers Town in northwest London with William Godwin
August 30, 1797
Gave birth to her second daughter, Mary
September 10, 1797
Died of puerperal fever (at age 38)
Her unfinished novel, "Maria", was published posthumously
January 1798
Her husband William Godwin published "Memoirs of the Author of A Vindication of the Rights of Woman"
"A Vindication of the Rights of Woman" (1792)

It is the first feminist philosophical text that advocates for women's right to education and equal fundamental rights, arguing that women are not naturally inferior to men but only appear to be so because they lack education.
